quote: The one-noun-at-a-time limit is the productive norm: Shibatani (1990: 72) states that two independently incorporable nouns cannot ordinarily stack. Nakagawa (2024: 488) records rare exceptions (cep-ya-o-kuta 'dump fish onto the shore', itak-mak-kus-te 'use hidden words'), which he places at the lexicalized fringe of the system rather than as evidence for productive double-NI.
issue: The examples 'cep-ya-o-kuta' and 'itak-mak-kus-te' appear to contain two nouns each (cep 'fish' + ya 'shore'; itak 'word' + mak 'hidden'), but the text claims they are exceptions to the one-noun-at-a-time limit. However, 'mak' is an adjective/adverb meaning 'hidden', not a noun, so 'itak-mak-kus-te' may not be a double-noun example. This undermines the claim about rare double-NI exceptions.
suggestion: Verify whether 'mak' is indeed a noun in this context, or rephrase to clarify that the exceptions involve noun+adverb or noun+noun combinations.

quote: Nakagawa (2024: 488) records rare exceptions (cep-ya-o-kuta 'dump fish onto the shore', itak-mak-kus-te 'use hidden words'), which he places at the lexicalized fringe of the system rather than as evidence for productive double-NI.
issue: The example 'cep-ya-o-kuta' contains the applicative prefix 'o-', which is an applicative, not a second noun. This example may involve noun+applicative+verb, not two nouns. The text's characterization as 'double-NI' is inconsistent with the earlier description of applicative-mediated incorporation.
suggestion: Clarify that 'cep-ya-o-kuta' involves an applicative prefix, making it a different structure from stacking two nouns.

quote: Nakagawa (2024: 480) notes that incorporation is commoner in verse than in prose or conversation; Ainu rarely incorporates two nouns into one verb.
issue: The claim 'Ainu rarely incorporates two nouns into one verb' directly contradicts the earlier statement (lines 189-201) that Nakagawa records rare exceptions of double-NI. If double-NI is already described as rare, this sentence is redundant and potentially contradictory if it implies a stronger prohibition.
suggestion: Either remove the redundant statement or clarify that it refers to productive double-NI (not lexicalized exceptions).

quote: Community discussion in the aynu-corpora Discord has noted that Ainu has very few pure-noun endocentric or exocentric compounds — constructions built solely from two nouns without a verbal head — with nominal formation concentrated on verb stems (antitwilight, aynu-corpora Discord, June 2026; <Ref k="aynucorporadiscord" />) ‹contested›.
issue: The claim is attributed to a single Discord user (antitwilight) and tagged ‹contested›, but the epistemic grade is appropriate. However, the citation format uses a generic reference key 'aynucorporadiscord' without a specific message link or date beyond the month. This is a citation hygiene issue as the source is not precisely locatable.
suggestion: Add a more specific citation (e.g., message ID or permalink) if available, or note that the reference is to a general discussion thread.

quote: This diagnostic has been observed in community practice with the pair kinakar=an (incorporated, intransitive) beside kina a=kar (phrasal, transitive with the fourth-person A prefix a=) for the colocation of kina and kar (thegodofneet, aynu-corpora Discord, December 2024; <Ref k="aynucorporadiscord" />). The observation directly corroborates Nakagawa (2024: 482–483) and Satō (2022, §2) ‹corpus-confirmed›.
issue: The evidence grade ‹corpus-confirmed› is applied to a claim supported only by a Discord message from a single user. This is an inflation of the epistemic grade, as 'corpus-confirmed' typically requires systematic corpus evidence, not a single community observation.
suggestion: Change to ‹corpus-suggested› or ‹speculative›, or provide additional corpus evidence.

quote: Baker (1988) derives from Case theory that unaccusative clauses cannot undergo applicative or P-incorporation, because the internal argument already fills the available Case position <Ref k="baker1988" paren />. Ainu falsifies this directly: applicatives attach freely to unaccusative bases, yielding objects that can then incorporate. Shibatani (1990: 66) cites mokor a=e-wen 'I missed sleeping' and sake a=e-niste 'I am strong at wine', both with e- on an unaccusative base, as the falsifying data <Ref k="shibatani1990" p="66" /> ‹contested›.
issue: The claim that Ainu falsifies Baker's prediction is tagged ‹contested›, but the preceding text presents it as a direct falsification without acknowledging that the contestation might come from alternative analyses (e.g., that the verbs in question are not truly unaccusative). The epistemic grade is appropriate, but the prose could be clearer about the nature of the contestation.
suggestion: Add a brief note on why the data are contested (e.g., 'some analysts question whether these verbs are unaccusative').

quote: The quasi-incorporation strategy discussed in §<Xr ch="noun-incorporation-oblique-polysynthesis-debate" s="markedness-qi" /> is a separate, structurally different response to the ceiling on NI — it handles cases where NI is grammatically blocked, not cases where noun stacking would otherwise be possible.
issue: The phrase 'not cases where noun stacking would otherwise be possible' is slightly awkward. It could be read as implying that QI handles cases where NI is blocked, but not cases where stacking would be possible (which is obvious). The intended meaning is likely 'not cases where noun stacking would otherwise be possible (i.e., it only applies when NI is blocked).'
suggestion: Rephrase to: '...it handles cases where NI is grammatically blocked, not cases where noun stacking would otherwise be possible (which are already handled by NI).'

quote: The systematic zero at the foot of the table arises because the inflectional possessor that body-part nouns require makes the incorporated noun definite and thereby removes the reflexive-rescue option.
issue: The phrase 'reflexive-rescue option' is not clearly defined earlier in the text. The table mentions 'Reflexive interpretation' as a rescue factor, but the connection to possessor-requiring nouns is not explained until the next sentence. The prose could be clearer.
suggestion: Add a brief explanation: '...removes the reflexive-rescue option (i.e., the reflexive interpretation that would otherwise reduce the markedness penalty).'

quote: Ainu rarely incorporates two nouns into one verb.
issue: This statement is redundant with the earlier discussion of the one-noun-at-a-time limit (lines 189-201). It also appears in a paragraph about register asymmetry, which is a different topic, making the sentence feel disconnected.
suggestion: Either remove the sentence or integrate it into the earlier discussion of stacking limits.

issue: The username 'antitwilight' is not italicized or formatted consistently with other usernames in the text (e.g., 'thegodofneet' is also not italicized, but this is inconsistent with the general practice of italicizing Ainu words). This is a minor formatting inconsistency.
suggestion: Either italicize all usernames or leave them unitalicized consistently. The current practice appears to be unitalicized, so no change needed, but it's worth noting.
